observations.recommendation: The PPE Asset Disposal and appraisal committee conduct an inspection and appraisal of all the unserviceable properties of the LGU. Thereafter, the Inventory and Inspection report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) be prepared as basis for the disposal and the dropping of the costs of the disposed properties in the books of the LGU, thus, present fairly the PPE and Governnment Equity accounts in the books.

We recommended that Management: Require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile his records of PPE with the physical inventory report, provide depreciation expenses for all depreciable PPE, and take up all necessary adjustments in the books, thus, establish the fair balance of the PPE in the Financial Statements; Require the Supply Officer and Accountant to maintain Property Cards (PC) and Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC), respectively and record the acquisition, description, custody, disposal and other information relative to the assets; Require the Supply Officer and Accountant to reconcile the inventory count report and the accounting records. Investigate and evaluate the differences, adjust the records as appropriate and submit a revised RPCPPE based on the reconciliations made.

We recommended that the: Accounting Office immediately stop the erroneous practice of recognizing outright expense for inventory items procured by the agency and use the appropriate inventory account in recording said stocks; GSO start preparing the required SCs and the maintenance of records of RIS and SSMI and regularly coordinate with the Accounting Office for purposes of reconciling of records; and GSO submit the report on the physical count of inventories within the period required in the above stated rule to the Audit Team.

We recommended and Management agreed that the concerned officials of the LGU should not tolerate payment of benefits or allowance to its official and employees which constitutes double compensation or irregular expenditures. The Municipal Accountant is directed to facilitate the collection of P10,000.00 from the concerned employee which should be made immediately and in full.

We recommended that the Management use the maximum monthly compensation of P5,000.00 per month as basis in computing the employer share as provided in RA 9679 or a maximum of P100.00 per month regardless of the amount that the employee contributes for the personal share, in conformity to the IRR of RA 9679.

We recommended and Management agreed to adhere to the provisions prescribed in the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 pertaining to the requirement of irrevocable standby letter of credit, bank guarantee or surety bond for all cash advances made to the contractors, otherwise, any expenses or loss in case the LGU failed to recover from the contractors may be charged to concerned approving officials.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct: a. The Legislative department endeavor to comply with the prescribed period of submission and approval of the Municipal Annual Budget under the Local Government Code of 1991; and b. The Local Development Council and the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO), together with the concerned implementing offices, in coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee, immediately look into the unimplemented projects under the 20% Development Fund and determine causes of their non-implementation. c. The offices concerned vigorously pursue the completion of the projects to be implemented the soonest time possible in order to achieve the objectives of the said projects in providing socio-economic benefits to the intended beneficiaries. d. The MPDO ensure that all identified unimplemented projects have the corresponding approval or authorization of the Sangguniang Bayan. e. The Municipal Engineering Office improve the planning and scheduling of work activities and manpower in order to facilitate completion of the Program of Work of unimplemented projects. f. The MPDO enhance monitoring of implementation of projects in coordination with the implementing offices and to submit regularly status reports of implementation to the Municipal Mayor for his information so that appropriate measures can be instituted to obtain optimal utilization of fund.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct: a. require the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) to review the existing PPAs under the LDRRMF to determine the relevance and responsiveness of the projects to the present circumstances including the reasonableness of project costing and make the necessary revisions as warranted subject to the appropriate approvals; b. direct the LDRRMO to strictly monitor the implementation of the PPAS to facilitate the timely completion thereof; and c. ensure that PPAs funded out of the DRRM funds are well-planned and meticulously reviewed by the LDRRMC.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive, Treasurer, Accountant and Budget Officer refrain from approving/allowing assistance to individuals/organizations that is not within the Municipality's responsibility to finance and/or subsidize. In the case of organizations conducting activities with a cause/outreach-programs, they should include supporting documents as proof that it was used as intended and/or there is an expected beneficial return to the Municipality and its constituents. Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant require a certification from the government agencies seeking financial assistance from LGU Maconacon. The certification should be provided by their authorized officer confirming that said activity has no appropriation and/or not funded by the agency. Likewise, the amount shall be appropriately acknowledged thru issuance of the agency's Official Receipt to ensure that such assistance is accordingly recorded in the recipients' books and will be used as intended. Lastly, refrain from granting financial assistance to national agencies wherein individual personnel were named as payees for purposes of check and balance.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct Bids and Awards Committee ensure all required postings as mentioned in the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 are complied with. Attach a printed copy of all postings to the related disbursement vouchers of these projects to prove compliance thereof.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly observe the issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance after the one-year defects liability period which shall be reckoned from the project completion in order not to expose the LGU Maconacon from risk of loss and in case its contractor failed to repair its defective infrastructure projects its warranty will benefit from performance security in case its contractor failed to repair its defective infrastructure projects.

We recommended and Management agreed that copy of contract and their supporting documents be submitted to the Office of the Auditor within five (5) days from the execution of the same in compliance with the above circular.

We recommended that Management should execute a MOA with TMI incorporating the scope of agreement and responsibilities of each party as provided under DC 2020-04-0008 dated April 6, 2020 with reference to DC 2018-08-0021 dated July 26, 2018 to ensure smooth implementation of ER 1-94 Funds for the management of COVID-19 emergency. If the required execution of MOA is not complied with, the related transaction arising from the utilization of the subject funds may be suspended in audit.
observations.managementAction: Management has not submitted a MOA that incorporates the  scope of agreement and responsibilities of each party as provided under DC 2020-04-0008 dated April 6, 2020 with reference to DC 2018-08-0021 dated July 26, 2018 to ensure smooth implementation of ER 1-94 Funds for the management of COVID-19 emergency.

instruct the Municipal Engineer’s Office, Bids and Awards Committee and its Secretariat to adhere to the conditions set forth in Section 54.1 of the procurement law that prohibits splitting of contracts/requisitions to evade or circumvent the requirements of law and 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184; and
stop the practice of contract splitting as this is disallowable in audit.

require the Accountant to submit the Disbursement Vouchers complete with their supporting documents; payrolls, and official receipts and other financial reports and documents within the prescribed period.
investigate and resolve the cause of delay in the submission of financial documents and reports by revisiting and improving the task assignment schedule of the accounting unit and prioritizing report preparation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Development Council (MDC) to ensure thorough deliberation of additional Projects/Programs and Activities (PPAs), including those sourced from re-aligned budgets in aid of the LGU’s effort to mitigate the impacts of COVID-19. Hence, this PPAs should be embodied in the local development plans to be properly endorsed to the Local Finance Committee for inclusion in the supplemental budget, together with the approved supplemental investment program.

We recommended that Management rectify the deficiency by instructing the focal personnel to facilitate the preparation of the AWP for the Year 2021 reflecting therein the projects that will be implemented using the remaining ER 1-94 Funds and ensure that the projects are in line with the guidelines of DOE Circular No. 2020-04-0008 dated April 6, 2020.

We recommended that Management should execute a MOA with TMI incorporating the scope of agreement and responsibilities of each party as provided under DC 2020-04-0008 dated April 6, 2020 with reference to DC 2018-08-0021 dated July 26, 2018 to ensure smooth implementation of ER 1-94 Funds for the management of COVID-19 emergency. If the required execution of MOA is not complied with, the related transaction arising from the utilization of the subject funds may be suspended in audit.

We recommended that Management obtain authority from the SB granting the LCE the permission to accept donations whether in cash or in-kind from local or foreign sources. Likewise, Management is required to cause the submission of resolutions, stating that the acceptance of donations received were authorized by the Sangguniang Bayan.

We recommended that Management instruct the Property/Supply Officer to adopt the use of prescribed forms indicated in applicable COA Circulars in order to ensure availability, reliability and quality of information that will be used in the preparation of mandatory reports as far as the receipt and utilization of resources intended for DRRM operations of the Municipality is concerned.
Moreover, management should render submission of the inventory of the remaining undistributed items and other reportorial requirements, where appropriate taking into consideration the applicable guidelines embodied in COA Circular 2020-009 dated April 21, 2020, otherwise the same shall be suspended in audit.

We recommended that Management observe the provisions embodied in GPPB Circular 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020 as prescribed under RA 11469 in the preparation of project requirements during emergency situations in order to facilitate smooth implementation of its procurement activities as planned.

We recommended that Management require the responsible personnel to render submission of purchaser’s and dealer’s permit from the NTC as well as the registration from the Secretary of Commerce Industry in order to establish the validity and legality of the transaction. Unless presented in audit, the transaction will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management instruct the MDRRMO to monitor the implementation of proposed programs, projects and activities which are funded by the unexpended balances of prior years’ LDRRMF. Likewise, it is imperative to ensure that funds should be utilized only for projects identified in the approved LDRRMF Investment Plan as embodied in the Supplemental AIP.

We recommended that the MDRRMO prepare the required LDRRMFIP to be approved by the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (MDRRMC) in order to regulate the utilization of unexpended LDRRMF thus ensuring the validity and propriety of programs, projects and activities implemented that build and strengthens resilience against disasters among the local community including the vulnerable and marginalized groups.

We recommended the following courses of actions to Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to observe the timely adjustment of all reconciling items determined during the preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statement to ensure accurate and correct Cash in Bank balance reflected in the books.
Require the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate reconciliation of the noted reconciling items, including those requiring adjustment by the bank. In case of failure to rectify the noted deficiencies, we invite your attention on paragraph 2 of Section 122 of PD 1445, relative to suspension of payment of salaries until the requirements of the Commission have been complied with.

We recommended that Management instruct the Accounting Unit and Property Custodian to take actions on the following:
Perform periodic reconciliation of property cards to ensure that all discrepancies are immediately investigated, cleared and reconciled with the books of accounts;
Prepare necessary journal entries to adjust the duly investigated reconciling items in order to reflect the correct account balances; and,
Maintain complete and accurate Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC) for each  category  of  plant,  property  and equipment, among others; maintain Property Cards (PC) for property, plant and equipment to account for the receipt and disposition of the same.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to observe the timely preparation and submission of the Bank Reconciliation Statements as provided under COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996. Likewise, follow up the enrolment in the LBP We-Access to aid the LGU in the timely preparation and regular submission of the monthly BRS.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Head of the Department concerned which is the Municipal Health Officer in this case or the authorized representative occupying a regular plantilla position, if any to render a report stating the volume of donated items utilized or the number of issuances to end-user units as basis for the Accounting Office to record the transactions relative to the utilization/distribution of the donated drugs, medicines and medical supplies that shall be treated as expenses for a given reporting date.
Unless the personnel-in-charge is able to provide a report on the receipt and utilization of donated drugs, medicines and medical supplies from DOH, he/she will be held liable for whatever pilferages or wastages incurred from non-compliance with the applicable laws, rules and regulations for property and supply management and that the related transactions shall likewise be suspended in audit.
Establish accounting requirements/guidelines or implementing rules and regulations that are deemed applicable in the storage, reporting and monitoring of donated drugs, medicines and medical supplies from DOH and/or other government agencies.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee undertake the following:
Submit minutes evidencing that a pre-procurement conference to determine the readiness of the Municipality to carry out the procurement project in terms of funds availability within the approved budget. Moreover, it shall adhere strictly to the minimum requirements of the law in holding such a procurement activity in the event that the contracts involved exceed the prescribed limit for goods and infrastructure projects.
Ensure that the Construction Safety and Health program forming an integral part of the contract should be marked as duly approved by the Department of Labor and Employment upon submission prior to the signing of contract, subject to the guidelines embodied in DOLE Department Order No. 13 series of 1998 and as required under applicable provisions in Section 37 of IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the officials of the Municipal Planning and Development Office and Municipal Budget Office install feasible mechanisms and appropriate measures in the adequate planning and monitoring of Projects, Programs and Activities to be included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) to maximize the benefits due to its beneficiaries.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Management to:

a) Implement Section 230 of the MRTC on dealing with the delinquent market vendors for the timely collection of market rentals and to prevent the eventual increase of uncollected surcharges.
observations.managementAction:
AAR
CY 2017 | The management was not able to collect all the delinquencies incurred by the concerned stall holders as at year end due to the non-imposition of the provisions of the Revised Municipal Revenue Code of 2018 especially in dealing with the delinquent market vendors.


At present, adjudication process on vacant stalls were properly applied and provisions of Revised Municipal Revenue Code of 2018 were simply interpreted and executed.
observations.reason:
The management adopted practice to collect delinquencies upon renewal of business permits and relying on the cash bond posted by the concerned delinquent stall holders as collaterals in case of non-payment of market stall rentals. This present system cost too much to the agency due to the following revelations:

One particular market vendor exceeded the amount of his delinquencies against the posted cash bond. (V. Tampepe)
Some delinquent vendors had not renewed their business licenses, leaving their monthly rentals unpaid as at year end. (C. Briz, S. Poblete, P. Cascabel).
Management allowed market vendors to occupy a space despite delinquent for months/years. (D. Orias, Maco Tough 40 Assoc.)

observations.recommendation: Instruct the personnel-in-charge of the trust liability accounts to conduct monthly reconciliation of same to determine the correct amounts that should be remitted within the prescribed period provided in the applicable rules and regulations so that the concerned government agencies could utilize the funds due them for their programmed activities and incurrence of penalties, interest and surcharges by the municipality relative thereto could be avoided.

If there are penalties and surcharges incurred for late remittances, the same should not be charged the funds of the municipality instead it should be treated as personal liability of the personnel responsible for the delay.

We recommended the management to:

1. 	Require the Municipal Accountant to recognize in full the RPT/SET Receivable at the beginning of the year based on the certified list of tax payers’ amount due and collectible for the year to be prepared by the Municipal Treasurer as basis for recording the amount in the books. The amount due and collectible should represent actual receivables arising from said taxes due  instead of income estimates to arrive at a fairly presented balances of the said accounts in the financial statements.


2. 	Require the Municipal Treasurer to record the estimated RPT/SET Receivables based on its year-end Schedule of Real Property Tax Delinquency as their target for collections in the current year.




3. 	Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile regularly the accounting and treasury records to verify the accuracy and validity of their records and to rectify promptly any discrepancies noted.
observations.managementAction:
The Accounting Office still partially recognized receivables from the RPT/SET  which was furnished by the Treasury Office. The management is hesitant to record in full its receivables on real properties considering some of these properties are not taxable.





The Treasury Office still followed the collection target set by the Bureau of Local Government Finance (BLGF)which was forwarded to the Accounting Office as basis in their recording of RPT/SEET receivables. Apparently, Management is hesitant to record in full its receivables on real properties due to its findings that some of these properties are not taxable.

During the year, the Accounting Office and the Treasury Office did not reconcile their records.
observations.reason:
Accordingly, management  cancelled some tax declaration whose properties are not subject to tax after these were identified as land under the Certificate of Stewardship Contracts, declared as Forestal Areas and Land Improvements. These cancelled real property units will eventually reduce the collectibles on real properties of the agency.





The Municipal Treasurer mentioned that the Bureau of Local Government Finance is responsible for setting the RPT target as basis in the determination of real property tax collectibles







Compliance with the law is less possible in terms reconciling records on real property taxes due to the following reasons:

a. Properties were either under Comprehensive Agrarian Reform Program (CARP) or court litigation like NGCP
b. Delinquent real property owners with unknown address
c.Declared owners which are no longer existing such as ADECOR, whose assessment was still in our records.
d. Unsurveyed lands (Forestal Zone) which were previously assessed by our Municipal Assessor, and up to now their record still exists.
e.Agricultural lands covered by Certificated of Stewardship Contract (CSC) issued by the DENR which were also previously assessed by our Municipal Assessor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Inventory Committee to convene and strategize the conduct of complete physical inventory of PPE to determine the actual existence of all assets owned by the Municipality. They are required  to  submit  the RPCPPE  on  or  before  January 31  of  each  year,  copy furnished the COA Auditor concerned pursuant to Section 124 of NGAS Manual, Volume I and Section 490 of GAAM, Volume I.;
Require the Municipal Accountant and Property Custodian to (i) maintain Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger   Card   (PPELC)   for   each   category   of   plant,   property   and equipment, among others; maintain Property Cards (PC) for property, plant and equipment to account for the receipt and disposition of the same (ii) reconcile their records for PPE accounts and determine the causes of the variance thereof and cause proper disposition; (ii) identify all PPE items without ARE and the employees accountable for the items to pave the way for the issuance of the ARE to all employees/officials concerned.

Require Municipal Accountant to effect proper adjustment on the affected PPE account and to adhere to the proper charging of depreciation based on the aforementioned standards.

We recommended to management the following:

observations.reason:
The management adopted practice  to collect delinquencies upon renewal of business permits and relying on the cash bond posted by the concerned delinquent stall holders as collaterals in case of non payment of market stall rentals. One particular market vendor exceeded the amount of his delinquencies against the posted cash bond. (V. Tampepe)
Some delinquent vendors had not renewed their business licenses leaving their monthly rentals unpaid as at year end. (C. Briz, S. Poblete, P. Cascabel).
Management allowed market vendors to occupy a space despite delinquent for months/years. (D. Orias, Maco Tough 40 Assoc.)

        presentation of the financial statements.

We recommend to limit the utilization of the 20% Development Fund to the programs and projects enumerated under DILG and DBM JMC No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011. Ensure that strict monitoring of the disbursements against the 20% Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: The management had planned and budgeted those projects which are allowed to be charged to the 20% Development Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommend to the Management to process only the payment of registration fees which are valid and have legal basis. Ensure that the travel of officials and employees are adequately supported with the necessary and correct documents in accordance with the provisions of PD 1445 and all applicable laws, rules, and regulations. Refund the amount double claimed pertaining to travel.
observations.managementAction: The management had already submitted the authority from DILG to undertake "On-Site Learning Exposure." However, the refund for the amount double claimed for such travel was not yet made by the concerned personnel as of December 31, 2016.
observations.reason: The Assistant Municipal Budget Officer had requested deferment on the full implementation of this recommendation because the management is still trying to address the issue with concerned personnel.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the agency should return immediately the residual funds received from the different national government agencies to the Bureau of Treasury as provided in the existing laws, rules, and regulations so that these will be used for other priority projects of the government. We also recommend that the Municipal Accounting Office monitor regularly whenever there are fund transfers from national government agencies and determine whether the specific purpose for which the funds are intended have been accomplished. Upon completion, any residual amount shall be returned at once to the source agency or reverted back to the national treasury, whichever is more appropriate, to be used for other purposes.
observations.managementAction: Out of the residual amount of P398,502.77, only the total amount of P24,234.54 was identified that belongs to the national government agencies of which, the amount of P18,916.95 was transferred already, leaving a balance of P5,317.59 to be remitted by CY 2017. Other balances were identified as agency funds representing counterpart funds for different projects as required and KKK funds. There are also funds that remained unverified due to the absence of some pertinent documents.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant justified that the retrieval of records that belong to previous years had cost them so much time to find. These records served as basis for the identification of the concerned accounts whether these funds are transferrable to the NGAs, LGU fund or a receivable.

observations.recommendation: Refund the amount illegally disbursed since it is not in accordance with DBM Budget Circular No. 2004-5A. Ensure that the payment of BAC honorarium should be in compliance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The concerned accountable officers already refunded the amount disallowed based on the issued Notice of Disallowances and were respectively issued with Notices of Settlement of Suspension/Disallowance/Charges (NSSDC) all dated July 7, 2016.

observations.recommendation: That the Municipal Government secure a Sangguniang Bayan Resolution, authorizing the creation of a special account in the General Fund for the recording of transactions relative to the received Share from National Wealth. The Municipal Accounting Office and Municipal Budget Office must ensure the proper maintenance of such special account in accordance with requirements set forth in RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government already maintained a special account in the General Fund for the receipt of its Share from National Wealth, as required in Section 313 of Republic Act (RA) 7160.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the management require the Municipal Accountant to provide the necessary information to strictly comply with current accounting standards especially on the presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Some accounts were already rectified by management but there are still accounts that did not disclose the necessary information and did not comply with the current accounting standards especially on the presentation of the financial statements as of December 31, 2016.
observations.reason: Reiterated as Finding No. 8 in this year's Annual Audit Report (AAR).

observations.recommendation: We recommend to strictly follow the guidelines on the attribution of the budget pursuant to Sections 4 (C.3 No. 2) and 4 (C.4 No. 5) of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01. Likewise, we also recommend that in case the agency is not trained in the administration of HGDG, they may seek assistance from gender analysis specialists/experts or from oversight agencies such as DILG, PCW, DBM, and NEDA.
observations.managementAction: The concerned GFPS was able to apply the guidelines on the attribution of the budget pursuant to Sections 4 (C.3 No. 2) and 4 (C.4 No. 5) of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 through the administration of HGDG for CY 2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommend to the Municipal GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to submit the consolidated GPBs of the Municipality to the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO) to ensure the alignment of the Municipality’s GAD PPAs to the priorities of the province, and then from PPDO to the DILG Regional Office for its review and approval within the schedule set under JMC No. 2013-01. Ensure also that a copy of the GAD AR, together with its complete supporting documents, are submitted to the COA Audit Team for proper evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The concerned GFPS had already submitted a copy of 2016 GAD Plan and Budget to the PPDO. A copy also was sent and reviewed by the DILG Provincial Office. Likewise, the 2016 GAD AR was also submitted to the Office of the Audit Team Leader.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant write a letter to the bank, demanding/requesting for the immediate release of the DM/CM supporting the Bank Statements. After these have been secured, promptly effect the necessary adjusting entries affecting the Cash in Bank account. Should the bank still fail to submit the said documents, kindly inform in writing the audit team concerned so that the team will be notified and be able to apply other procedures necessary to aid the agency in the preparation of BRS.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had already submitted the required documents to the Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP), Tagum City Branch for them to submit to their main office in Manila. However, she was instructed lately, that she should submit again the same documents for they can provide the requested debit and credit memos on their own level.
observations.reason: The management received a delayed information on the re-submission of the required documents from LBP Tagum City Branch which resulted to the non-submission of the same as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Government direct the Municipal Property Inventory Team to conduct a complete physical inventory of all properties owned by the Municipality at least once a year and prepare inventory report thereof in compliance with Section 124 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs – Report on the Physical Count of Plant, Property and Equipment (RCPPE). The report must be properly accomplished and copy of such must be furnished to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation on purposes.
observations.managementAction: The Property Inventory Team had already conducted the physical count of their Property, Plant and Equipment for CY 2016.
observations.reason: The submitted 2016 Inventory Report was not reconciled with the records of the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Government strictly follow the guidelines on the attribution of the budget in accordance with Sections 4 (C.3 No. 2) and 4 (C.4 No. 5) of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2013-01. Likewise, we also recommend that in case the agency is not trained in the administration of HGDG, they may seek assistance from gender analysis specialists/experts or from oversight agencies such as DILG, PCW, DBM, and NEDA.
observations.managementAction: The concerned GFPS was able to apply the guidelines on the attribution of the budget pursuant to Sections 4 (C.3 No. 2) and 4 (C.4 No. 5) of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 through the administration of HGDG for CY 2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Government allocate funds for the acquisition of lot for the operation and management of Sanitary Landfill for final waste disposal and for the purchase of additional equipment for collection of wastes to sustain the effective implementation of the Solid Waste Management Plan. Prioritization of the funding for the purchase of these properties in the Supplemental Budget should be immediately done so that the delivery of social services in terms of collection and disposal of waste would not be hampered.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2016, the management was able to allocate in their budget the amount of P500,000.00 for the purchase of lot intended for Sanitary Landfill. The management also budgeted for additional equipment but the amount was insufficient.
observations.reason: The budgeted amount of P500,000.00 is not sufficient for the purchase of lot intended for Sanitary Landfill. Likewise, no budget was allocated for the purchase of additional equipment for collection of wastes for CY 2016.

observations.recommendation: The Local Chief Executive should direct the Municipal Property Inventory Team to conduct a complete physical inventory of all properties owned by the Municipality at least once a year, prepare inventory report thereof using the prescribed form – Report on the Physical Count of Plant, Property and Equipment (RCPPE), and furnish the Municipal Accountant a copy of the report for reconciliation purposes. All unserviceable equipment or properties should be included in the physical count. Then a report of all unserviceable properties using the prescribed form – Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) should be prepared and be submitted to the Office of the COA Auditor, together with the request of the Local Chief Executive for inspection and appraisal. This will serve as basis for possible disposal and/or dropping from the books of accounts. Further, additional personnel should be assigned both at the Offices of the MGSO and of the Municipal Accountant that would focus solely in maintaining and updating property cards and property ledger cards, respectively. Periodic reconciliation on of the involved records in said Offices should be made so that necessary adjustments thereof, if there are any, would then be effected in the books of the LGU in order to present fairly the PPE accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Property Inventory Team had already conducted the physical count of their Property, Plant and Equipment for CY 2016.
observations.reason: The submitted 2016 Inventory Report was not reconciled with the records of the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: Require the Accountable Officers concerned to follow strictly the provisions of Sections 4.1 and 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002. Cash advances granted for local travels should be liquidated within thirty (30) days after return to station, respectively. Since Notices of Suspensions had been issued to the Accountable Officers concerned relative thereto, submission of the liquidation documents of the unliquidated cash advances should be made within ninety (90) days from receipt hereof, otherwise, the P648,496.00 total suspended amount shall become disallowance after lapse of the prescribed period. Further, advise those Accountable Officers who were also issued with demand letters during the year 2013 for their unliquidated cash advances as of September 30, 2013 balances but were not yet issued with Notice of Suspensions (NS) as of December 31, 2013, to immediately settle their outstanding cash advances, otherwise, another batch of NS will be served to them by the audit team in the ensuing year for their failure to liquidate their long outstanding cash advances, despite receipt of the demand letters.
observations.managementAction: The management was able to reduce the cash advance through various liquidations made by the concerned accountable officers from P648,496.00 to P65,864.00 registering 89.85% decrease as at year end.
observations.reason: The management did not send Demand Letters to all concerned personnel for CY 2016.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the personnel-in-charge of the trust liability accounts to conduct monthly reconciliation on of same to determine the correct amounts that should be remitted within the prescribed period provided in the applicable rules and regulations so that the concerned government agencies could utilize the funds due them for their programmed activities and the incurrence of penalties, interest and surcharges by the municipality relative thereto could be avoided. If there are penalties and surcharges incurred for late remittances, the same should not be charged the funds of the municipality instead it should be treated as personal liability of the personnel responsible for the delay.
observations.managementAction: For 2013 balances of Pag-ibig Fund, Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR), GSIS and Philhealth, most of these were already remitted as of December 31, 2016.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant averred that they are still reconciling the remaining amount as to whom these unremitted balances are to be credited.

observations.recommendation: Comply strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 to ensure proper granting, utilization and liquidation of the cash advances. The Accountable Officer concerned especially of those unliquidated cash advances which were granted in 2011 and in earlier years should make immediate liquidation thereof on or before January 31, 2013 so that the afore mentioned consequences as provided in Item 9.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-004 issued on November 18, 2012 re: Demand for Immediate Liquidation and Settlement of All Cash Advances Outstanding as of December 31, 2011 and that of the amended Section 8, Rule V of CSC Resolution dated June 17, 2004 will be avoided. Refrain from granting additional cash advances unless the previous ones were properly accounted and settled. Exhaust all means to require the Accountable Officers who have been separated from the service to either refund or liquidate their cash advances. Constant follow-up should be made with the COA Central Office, Manila regarding the issuance of the credit notices for the liquidation of the Intelligence Fund (IF) liquidations which were already transmitted to said Office or if necessary the AO concerned should personally go to COA Central Office to facilitate the issuance of same.
observations.managementAction: The management made several follow-ups in COA Central Office in 2016 for the issuance of Credit Notices for the liquidation of Cash Advance for Intelligence Fund but was only expressly assured that the same will be issued sooner to the municipality.
observations.reason: The management was only expressly assured by the COA Central Office that the Credit Notices will be issued sooner to Municipality.

observations.recommendation: Direct the Municipal Treasurer to intensify the collection of receivables using the billing statements from the Accounting Department in order to recover government funds and utilize collection to finance the present thrusts and priorities of the Municipality. Sit down and confer together in order to discuss matters of collections and payments to finally settle accounts. The receivable accounts should from time be evaluated and monitored on whether or not these can still be collected. If collection of same is already remote, prepare a Schedule attached with required supporting documents and request the Sangguniang Bayan to enact resolution seeking authority from the Commission on Audit for its possible write-off from the books of the municipality. If approved, effect the necessary adjustments to avoid the overstatement of the affected asset accounts and the corresponding equity account in the books of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: Out of P11,140,763.27, the total amount of P3,789,776.59 is attributed to Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable of which upon verification of these accounts for CY 2016, actual collections exceeded the agency’s estimated collections. As to the remaining amount of P7,350,986.68, several collections and adjustment were made totalling P3,350,069.06 as of December 31, 2016 involving accounts 'Advances to Officers and Employees', 'Due From Other Funds', 'Receivable-Disallowances/Charges', and 'Other Receivables'. The management is also considering the writing-off of the total amounts in accounts 'Due From NGAs', 'Due From LGUs', 'Due From NGOs/POs' and part of Other Receivables in the total amount of P780,490.62.
observations.reason: Credit Notices were not yet received for the liquidation of Cash Advance granted in previous years for Intelligence Funds by COA Central Office. Some documents were lost for loans extended to some debtors who presently claimed that what they received was a form of a grant from the municipal government. Some accountable officers were not already connected with the agency as of December 31, 2016.

observations.recommendation: Give utmost priority to the titling of the lots which are utilized by the municipality in order to ensure ownership over the said lands and to avoid possible damage to government properties by unwanted transfer through eviction.
observations.managementAction: The agency claimed that all the necessary documents for the titling of lots were already submitted to respective agencies where these are accordingly being processed.
observations.reason: No follow-up was made after the submission of pertinent documents to concerned agencies.
